metadata.title: Why 'Alpha Radiation Is Stronger' Is the Wrong Reason to Pick It
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine candidate 07. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/07-radioligand-theranostics.md (emitter range vs LET vs crossfire and geometry-matching punchline only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = beta emitter / crossfire / correct match for heterogeneous tumor, crimson = alpha confined / geometry mismatch, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = beta emitter crossfire / correct match for heterogeneous tumor; CRIMSON #BF3339 = alpha confined to one cell / geometry mismatch; GOLD #F5D061 = the range comparison highlight (fill only, never text); never swap mid-film
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: PSMA pair, image-then-treat loop, absorbed-dose/organ-toxicity, actinium supply chain. Keep only range vs LET vs crossfire and the geometry-matching punchline.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A patient has a bulky, heterogeneous tumor. Alpha radiation delivers far more lethal energy per hit than beta. The oncologist picks beta anyway. Here is why that is not a mistake.

card.copy: Why 'Alpha Radiation Is Stronger' Is the Wrong Reason to Pick It
card.sub: matching the emitter to the tumor's geometry

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] alpha is stronger — oncologist picks beta

beats.narration_text: Alpha particles are helium nuclei — heavy and fast. In fifty to a hundred micrometers of tissue, they shred DNA with dense, clustered double-strand breaks. These are the kind of breaks the cell's repair machinery genuinely struggles to fix.

production_viz.label: alpha: dense short track, extreme LET
production_viz.mechanic: a bound cell (SLATE filled circle) with a short dense crimson track emanating right — labeled 'Alpha particle' in CRIMSON and 'range 0.05-0.1 mm' in MONO
production_viz.note: introduce alpha in isolation first — the intimidating one

beats.narration_text: Beta particles travel several millimeters. The damage is sparse and spread thin — each individual hit is survivable. And yet for most clinical solid tumors, this is the emitter oncologists reach for. That is worth understanding.

production_viz.label: beta: long sparse track, low LET
production_viz.mechanic: a bound cell (SLATE circle) with a long thin teal track extending far right — labeled 'Beta particle' in TEAL and 'range 1-2 mm' in MONO
production_viz.note: beta introduced — seems weaker, but setup for the payoff

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] beta track: long, sparse, reaches far

beats.narration_text: Why would a drug company spend years developing 'weaker' radiation for a disease where you need to kill every cancer cell? The answer isn't in the particle. It's in the tumor's geometry.

card.copy: Alpha delivers far more lethal energy per hit than beta. For many cancers, oncologists pick the 'weaker' beta emitter anyway. Why would less lethal radiation be the better choice?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] the gap formula — on screen and in narration

beats.narration_text: Here's what range actually means inside a real tumor. Alpha: fifty to a hundred micrometers — a few cell widths. The particle stays inside the cell that bound the drug, maybe reaching one immediate neighbor. After that, it stops.

production_viz.label: split panel — alpha range vs beta range
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side panels. Left: alpha panel — bound cell (SLATE circle) with a short dense crimson track, dashed line showing 0.05-0.1mm boundary, neighbor cells faded. Right: beta panel — bound cell with a long teal track extending 1-2mm into neighbor cells (teal faded circles). Labels show range values in MONO.
production_viz.note: the core range comparison — the manim move: split

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] side-by-side range comparison — alpha confined, beta reaches neighbors

beats.narration_text: Beta: one to two millimeters. That crosses dozens to a hundred cell lengths. A cell that never bound the drug still gets irradiated because its neighbor did. That is crossfire — and here's where it gets interesting — it is not a flaw. It is exactly the feature you need.

production_viz.label: crossfire — beta radiation reaches target-negative cells
production_viz.mechanic: a row of mixed cells: some SLATE (receptor-positive, bound drug), some with no binding (receptor-negative, lighter). The beta track from a bound cell sweeps through multiple cells including the negative ones. Teal arrows/tracks show irradiation of neighbors. Label 'crossfire' in TEAL.
production_viz.note: crossfire is the key mechanism — this is the 'feature not bug' beat

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] crossfire — beta track from bound cell irradiates target-negative neighbors

beats.narration_text: Now the geometry problem. Real tumors are not uniform. A three-centimeter neuroendocrine tumor has receptor-positive cells on its rim and a receptor-negative core. The drug binds the rim. No drug reaches the center.

props.headline: Now the geometry problem. Real tumors are not uniform. A three-centimeter…

beats.scene_description: a schematic cross-section of a heterogeneous tumor — the outer ring bright (receptor-positive, teal-tinted), the cold core a different shade (receptor-negative, muted), illustrating the geometry mismatch; editorial newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B07, cross-section schematic of a heterogeneous solid tumor 3cm diameter, outer ring labeled receptor-positive (bright teal highlight), cold interior core labeled receptor-negative (muted gray-blue), scientific diagram style, flat editorial illustration, newsprint desaturated treatment, no text labels on the image itself, center-pan camera
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] heterogeneous tumor cross-section — rim positive, core negative

beats.narration_text: An alpha emitter bound to the rim kills those cells with extreme efficiency. But the fifty-micrometer range never crosses the gap to the core. The receptor-negative cells in the center survive, untouched. You have killed the shell and left the interior intact.

production_viz.label: alpha on heterogeneous tumor — rim killed, core survives
production_viz.mechanic: simplified tumor cross section: outer ring of SLATE cells with crimson tracks (alpha, bound to rim), inner core cells with no tracks. A dashed boundary arc shows the 0.05-0.1mm reach. Core cells shown fading in to illustrate survival. Label 'core survives' in crimson.
production_viz.note: alpha's precision is its weakness here — confined track cannot cross to core

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] alpha on heterogeneous tumor — rim cells killed, core untouched

beats.narration_text: Now put a beta emitter on the same tumor. The rim cells absorb more drug. Their beta tracks travel one to two millimeters inward — crossing the gap and irradiating the core. Crossfire doing work that the drug itself can never do.

production_viz.label: beta on heterogeneous tumor — crossfire reaches the core
production_viz.mechanic: same tumor cross section: rim cells (SLATE) with long teal beta tracks extending inward — crossing the boundary arc and irradiating core cells. Core cells show teal irradiation. Label 'crossfire reaches core' in TEAL.
production_viz.note: this is the geometry-matching payoff — beta wins because crossfire

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] beta crossfire reaches the receptor-negative core — the geometry match

beats.narration_text: The question is never which isotope is more lethal per hit. It is which range matches the spatial distribution of target expression in this patient's tumor. Geometry is the deciding variable — not raw damage per track.

card.copy: The question is not which emitter hits harder.
card.sub: It is which range matches the tumor's geometry.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] the implication — geometry, not raw lethal force

beats.narration_text: Take a three-centimeter neuroendocrine tumor with heterogeneous SSTR2 expression. Lu-177 with beta-range crossfire from bound cells: roughly seventy-eight percent tumor cell kill in this illustrative case. Ac-225 with alpha-only precision: receptor-negative neighbors survive — about forty-one percent kill. The weaker emitter wins because its range fits the tumor's geometry.

production_viz.label: illustrative example — Lu-177 vs Ac-225 on heterogeneous NET
production_viz.mechanic: two StateCard-style panels side by side. Left: 'Lu-177 (beta)' slate card with '78%' kill figure in TEAL. Right: 'Ac-225 (alpha)' slate card with '41%' kill figure in CRIMSON. Below each card a small row of isotype squares colored to show kill proportion. Footer label 'illustrative numbers'.
production_viz.note: the worked example — numbers are illustrative, labeled as such. SSTR2-positive tumor, heterogeneous geometry.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] illustrative example — Lu-177 78% vs Ac-225 41% on heterogeneous NET

beats.narration_text: More lethal per hit. Less useful in the tumor. Geometry decides which emitter wins — and the answer changes with every patient's scan.

card.copy: More lethal per hit does not mean more useful in the tumor.
card.sub: Geometry decides which emitter wins. — CANCER NANOMEDICINE
